All particles, regardless of their size, have a de Broglie wavelength.
The de Broglie wavelength of macroscopic objects can be observed with standard laboratory equipment.
explanation: Statement  A  is true as every object has a de Broglie wavelength, which can be calculated using the de Broglie equation . However, Statement B is false because the de Broglie wavelength of macroscopic objects is so small that it is not detectable with standard laboratory equipment, as their mass makes the wavelength extremely tiny. Correct option is (2)

question: A photon released by the transition of an electron from the second excited state to the ground state of Hydrogen atom is incident on the surface of a metal of work function 3.1 $e V$. The de-Broglie wavelength of the most energetic electron emitted from that metal surface is nearly
explanation:
Energy of photon released by the transition of an electron from second excited state ( $n_{2}=3$ ) to ground state ( $n_{1}=1$ ) is given as
$\Delta E=h v=E_{3}-E_{1}$
$=-\dfrac{13.6}{3^{2}}-\left(-\dfrac{13.6}{1^{2}}\right) {eV}=\dfrac{8}{9} \times 13.6 {eV}$
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation
$(K E)_{\text {max }}=h v-\phi_{0}$
$=\dfrac{8}{9} \times 13.6-3.1 \simeq 10 {eV}$
Now de-Broglie wavelength of the most energetic electron,
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2m(KE)} }}$ $ = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{\sqrt {2 \times 9.31 \times {{10}^{31}} \times 10 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}} }}$
$ = 4.09 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,m \simeq 4$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated between two plates having a potential difference of  $900{\text{ }}V$ is nearly
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron accelerated with a potential difference $V$ is given by
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}}$
where $E=K . E$. acquired by the electron.
Now, $E=e V$ ( $e$ is charge, $V$ is potential difference)
$\therefore \lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2meV} }}{\text{ }}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Putting the value of $h, m, e$ in the equation 1
$\lambda=\dfrac{1.23}{\sqrt{V}} {~nm}$
Now, put value of potential difference which is $900{\text{ }}V$ given
$l=\dfrac{1.23}{\sqrt{900}} {~nm}=\dfrac{1.23}{30} {~nm} \Rightarrow \lambda=0.04 {~nm}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle moving with a speed of $0.8 c$ is equal to the wavelength of a photon. If $c$ is speed of the photon in vacuum, the ratio of the energy of the photon and the kinetic energy of the particle is
explanation:
Given speed of moving particle
$v=0.8 c$
We know that, de-Broglie wavelength associated with moving particle
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \lambda = \frac{h}{{m \times 0.8c}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Energy of photon, $E=\dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$
Kinetic energy of particle
$E^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m \cdot(0.8 c)^{2}=0.32 m c^{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{E}{E^{\prime}}=\dfrac{h c / \lambda}{0.32 m c^{2}}=\dfrac{h}{\lambda(0.32 m c)}$
$=\dfrac{h}{\dfrac{h}{m \times 0.8 c} \times 0.32 m c}$ (from Eq. (1))
$=\dfrac{0.8}{0.32}=\dfrac{5}{2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron, accelerated through a potential difference of $121 V$ is about (take, Planck's constant $=h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} J s$, mass of electron $=9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}$)
explanation:
Given, potential difference, $V=121 {~V}$
$h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} J-s, m=9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}$
$K=$ Kinetic energy acquired by the electron
$=e V$
de-Broglie wavelength is given as
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}$
$\lambda=\dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 121}} m$
$=0.112 \times 10^{-9} {~m}=0.112 {~nm}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron with kinetic energy of 320 $eV$ is (take, $h=6.0 \times 10^{-34} S I$ unit, mass of electron$=m_{e}=9.0 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}$, charge of an electron$=1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}$ )
explanation:
Given, kinetic energy of electron, $K = 320\,eV$ $ = 320 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J = 512 \times {10^{ - 17}}J$
$h=6 \times 10^{-34} J-s$
Mass of electron, $m_{e}=9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}$, charge of electron $e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} C$
$\therefore$ de-Broglie wavelength of electron,
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}=\dfrac{6 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 512 \times 10^{-17}}} m$
$=\dfrac{6 \times 10^{-34}}{9.6 \times 10^{-24}} {~m}$
$=0.625 \times 10^{-10} {~m}=62.5 \times 10^{-12} {~m}=62.5 {pm}$
$\left[\because 1 {pm}=10^{-12} {~m}\right]$
So, the correct option is (3).

question: Which of the following has the largest de-Broglie wavelength?
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength of a moving particle is given as
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{m v} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{m v}$
Since, product of $m v$ for a ball of mass $0.03{\text{ }}kg$ moving with velocity $1 {~m} / {s}=1 \times 0.03$
$=0.03 {~kg} {~m} / {s}$ which is minimum among the all given options. Therefore, de-Broglie wavelength of ball of mass $0.03{\text{ }}kg$ moving with velocity $1 {~m} / {s}$ is largest.
So, the correct option is (4).

question: Which of the following particles has the shortest de-Broglie wavelength?
explanation:

So, the correct option is (3).

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron having kinetic energy 100 $eV$ is (Take,  $h = 4.14 \times {10^{ - 15}}eV - s,$  mass of electron $ = \frac{{0.5 \times {{10}^6}}}{{{c^2}}}eV,1pm = {10^{ - 12}}\,m)$
explanation:
Given, kinetic energy, $E=100 {eV}$
Planck's constant, $h=4.14 \times 10^{-15} {eV}-s$
Mass of electron, $m_{e}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{c^{2}} {eV}$
$1 p m=10^{-12} m$
If $p$ be the momentum of the electron, then
$\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m_{e}}=K \cdot E=E \Rightarrow p=\sqrt{2 m_{e} E}$
$\therefore$ de Broglie wavelength is,
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} E}}$
$\therefore \lambda=\dfrac{4.14 \times 10^{-15}}{\sqrt{2 \times \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{c^{2}} \times 100}} m$
$=\dfrac{4.14 \times 10^{-15} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{\sqrt{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{8}}} m$
$=124.2 \times 10^{-12} {~m}=124.2 {pm}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the first Bohr orbit of the hydrogen atom is
explanation:
According to de-Broglie hypothesis, revolving electron in circular orbit shows wave nature. Hence, a circular orbit can be taken to be a stationary energy state only if it contains an integral number of de-Broglie wavelength, i. e. we must have,
$2 \pi r_{n}=n \lambda$
where, $r_{n}=$ radius of ${n^{{\text{th}}}}$ orbit
and $n=$ principal quantum number of stationary state.
For first orbit, $n=1 \Rightarrow 2 \pi r=\lambda$.
So, de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is equal to the circumference of first orbit.
So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: equal to the diameter of the first orbit
equal to the circumference of the first orbit
equal to the half circumference of the first orbit
independent of the size of the first orbit

subject: Physics

question: A particle of charge $q$, mass $m$ and energy $E$ has de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda$. For a particle of charge $2 q$, mass $2 m$ and energy $2 E$, the de-Broglie wavelength is
explanation:
The de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ of a moving particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ and energy $E$ is given by
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mE} }}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
For another particle mass $m^{\prime}=2 m$,
charge $q^{\prime}=2 q$ and energy, $E^{\prime}=2 E$,
$\therefore$ de-Broglie wavelength,
$\lambda^{\prime}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\prime} E^{\prime}}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 \times 2 m \times 2 E}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}} \Rightarrow \lambda^{\prime}=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron of kinetic energy 9 $eV$ is (take,  $h = 4 \times {10^{ - 15}}eV - s,c = 3 \times {10^{10}}\,cm/s$  and the  mass $m_{e}$ of electron as  ${m_e}{c^2} = 0.5MeV)$
explanation:
If $p$ be the momentum of a moving electron, then
$\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m_{e}}=K E \Rightarrow p=\sqrt{2 m_{e}(K E)}$
$\therefore p c=\sqrt{2(K E) \times m_{e} c^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow p c=\sqrt{2 \times 9 e V \times 0.5 \times 10^{6}}$
$\Rightarrow p c=3 \times 10^{3} {eV}$
Now, de-Broglie wavelength,
$\lambda=\dfrac{h c}{p c}=\dfrac{4 \times 10^{-15} \times 3 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{3}}=4 \times 10^{-8} {~cm}$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: If a proton is accelerated through a potential difference of  $1000{\text{ }}V,$  then its de-Broglie wavelength is (given $m_{p}=1.67 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$, $h=6.63 \times 10^{-34} J-s$ )
explanation:
When a proton is accelerated through a $p\,.\,d.\,V,$ its de Broglie wave length is,
${\lambda _{{\text{proton}}}} = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{{0.286}}{{\sqrt V }}$ ($ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $)
$\therefore \quad \lambda=\dfrac{0.286}{\sqrt{1000}} \times 10^{-10} m \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-13} m$
Correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A photon and an electron (mass $m$) have the same energy $E$. The ratio ($\lambda_{\text {photon }} / \lambda_{\text {electron }}$) of their de Broglie wavelengths is: ($c$ is the speed of light)
explanation:
For photon, $E=\dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{P h}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{P h}=\dfrac{h c}{E}$
For electron, $p=$ momentum and
$E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\left(\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{e}}\right)^{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2 m} \Rightarrow \lambda_{e}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\lambda_{P h}}{\lambda_{e}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{h c}{E}}{\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}}=c \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m}{E}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron is the same as that of a photon. If velocity of electron is $25 \%$ of the velocity of light, then the ratio of $K.E.$ of electron and  $K.E.$ of photon will be
explanation:
$\lambda_{e}=\lambda_{\text {photon }}$
Velocity of electron $=25 \%$ velocity of light
Kinetic energy of electron
$=E_{k}=\dfrac{1}{2} p v_{e}=\dfrac{h v_{e}}{2 \lambda_{e}}\left[\because p=\dfrac{h}{\lambda}\right]$
and $K . E$. of photon $=\dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{p}}$
$\therefore$ Ratio $=\dfrac{\text { K.E. of electron }}{\text { K.E. of photon }}=\dfrac{h v_{e}}{2 \lambda_{e}} \dfrac{\lambda_{p}}{h c}$
(Given, $\lambda_{e}=\lambda_{p}$ )
$ = \frac{{{v_e}}}{{2\,c}} = \frac{{0.25\,c}}{{2\,c}} = \frac{{25}}{{200}} = \frac{1}{8}\quad \left( {{v_e} = 0.25\,c} \right)$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a proton and an $\alpha$-particle are $\lambda$ and $2 \lambda$ respectively. The ratio of the velocities of proton and $\alpha$-particle will be
explanation:
$\lambda_{P}=\lambda, \lambda_{\alpha}=2 \lambda$
The de-Broglie's theorem
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{m v}$
$m_{P}=m_{e}, m_{\alpha}=4 m_{e}$
$\dfrac{\lambda_{P}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\dfrac{m_{\alpha} v_{\alpha}}{m_{P} v_{P}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \lambda}=\dfrac{4 m \cdot v_{\alpha}}{m \cdot v_{P}}$
$\dfrac{1}{2}=4 \dfrac{v_{\alpha}}{v_{P}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{P}}{v_{\alpha}}=\dfrac{8}{1}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A proton, an electron and an alpha particle have the same energies. Their de-Broglie wavelengths will be compared as
explanation:
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}$
$\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$ or $K$ is constant
Since, $m_{e} < m_{p} < m_{\alpha}$
$\therefore \quad {\lambda _e} > {\lambda _p} > {\lambda _\alpha } \Rightarrow {\lambda _\alpha } < {\lambda _p} < {\lambda _e}$
subject: Physics

question: A proton and an electron have the same de-Broglie wavelength. If $K_{p}$ and $K_{e}$ be the kinetic energies of proton and electron respectively, then choose the correct relation.
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength,
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}$. If $\lambda$ is same, so momentum $p$ will be same.
Now, kinetice energy $K=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$
$\therefore K \propto \dfrac{1}{m} ; m_{p}>m_{e} \Rightarrow K_{p} < K_{e}$

question:
A proton and an electron are associated with same de-Broglie wavelength. The ratio of their kinetic energies is
(Assume $h = 6.63 \times {10^{ - 34}}Js,$ ${m_e} = 9.0 \times {10^{ - 31}}\,kg$ and ${m_p} = 1836\,\,{\text{times}}\,\,{m_e})$
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength, $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K E}}$
For same wavelength, $m K E=$ same
$\Rightarrow m_{P} K E_{P}=m_{e} K E_{e}$
$\dfrac{K E_{p}}{K E_{e}}=\dfrac{m_{e}}{m_{p}}=1: 1836$

question: Calculate de-Broglie wavelength  for an average helium atom in a furnace at $400\,K.$ Given mass of helium $ = 4.002\,amu.$
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength, $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}$
Mean $KE$ of helium atom $=\dfrac{3 k T}{2}$
or $E=\dfrac{3 \times\left(1.38 \times 10^{-23}\right) \times 400}{2}$
In terms of momentum, the energy is given by $E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$
or $\quad p^{2}=2 m E$
or $p=\sqrt{2 m E}$
$\therefore \quad p = \sqrt {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}{2 \times \left( {4.002 \times 1.66 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}} \right) \times 3 \times }\\{\left( {1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}}} \right) \times 200}\end{array}} $
(where mass of helium $4.000\,amu = 4.002 \times 1.66 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg\,)$
Now, $\lambda=\dfrac{6.625 \times 10^{-34}}{p}$
Substituting the value of $p$ and solving it, we get
$\lambda=0.63 \times 10^{-10} {~m}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron of mass ${m}$ when accelerated through a potential difference ${V}$, has de Broglie wavelength ${\lambda}$. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with a particle of mass ${m / 4}$ and charge ${e}$ accelerated through the same potential difference is ${n \lambda}$. Then find the value of ${n}$.
explanation:
Momentum of electron, $p_{e}=\sqrt{2 m e V}$
Momentum of particle, $p_{p}=\sqrt{2 \dfrac{m}{4} \cdot {eV}}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{e}}=\dfrac{h / p_{p}}{h / p_{e}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{m / 4}}=2$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The radius of an ${\alpha}$-particle moving in a circle in a constant magnetic field is half of the radius of an electron moving in circular path in the same field. The de-Broglie wavelength of ${\alpha}$-particle is ${n}$ times that of the electron. Find ${n}$ (an integer).
explanation:
$r \propto \dfrac{p}{q} \quad\left(\right.$ since, $\left.r=\dfrac{p}{B q}\right)$
where $p=$ momentum, Given $r_{\alpha}=\dfrac{1}{2} r_{e}$
$\dfrac{p_{\alpha}}{B 2 e}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{p_{e}}{B e}\right)$
or $p_{\alpha}=p_{e}$
$\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{p} \quad\left(\text { since, } \lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}\right)$
So, $\lambda_{\alpha}=\lambda_{e}$
or $n=1$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question:
Calculate the ratio of the accelerating potential required to accelerate (i) a proton and (ii) an ${\alpha}$-particle to have the same de-Broglie wavelength associated with them.
[Given: Mass of proton ${=1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}}$; Mass of ${\alpha}$-particle ${\left.=6.4 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}\right]}$
explanation:
If a particle is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, then
$q V=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$ or $p=\sqrt{2 m q V}$
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}$
As $\lambda_{p}=\lambda_{\alpha}$
or $\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} q_{p} V_{p}}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{a} q_{a} V_{\sigma}}}$
or $\dfrac{V_{p}}{V_{a}}=\dfrac{m_{a} q_{a}}{m_{p} q_{p}}=\dfrac{4 m_{p} \cdot 2 e}{m_{p} \cdot e}=8$.
subject: Physics

question: According to de-Broglie, the de-Broglie wavelength for electron in an orbit of hydrogen atom is ${10^{-9} {~m}}$. What is the principal quantum number for this electron?
explanation:
$2\pi rn = \lambda \Rightarrow n = \frac{l}{{2\pi r}}$
$ = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 9}}}}{{2 \times 3.14 \times 5.13 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}}} = 3$

question: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron moving with a velocity of $2 \times 10^{8} {~m} {~s}^{-1}$ is equal to that of a photon. Find the ratio of the kinetic energy of the photon to that of the electron.
explanation:
Speed of photon $\left( c \right) = 3 \times {10^8}\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$
Let $\lambda$ be the wavelength of the photon.
$\therefore \lambda=\dfrac{{h}}{{mc}}$ also, $\lambda_{{e}}=\dfrac{{h}}{{m}_{{e}} {v}}$ and $\lambda=\lambda_{{e}}$
Now, $\frac{{{\rm{ K}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{. of \,photon }}}}{{{\rm{ K}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{. of\, electron }}}} = \frac{{hv}}{{(1/2){m_e}{v^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{2hc}}{{{m_e}{v^2}\lambda }}$
$ = \frac{{2c}}{v}\quad \,\,\,\,\left( {\because {\lambda _e} = \frac{h}{{{m_e}v}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{2 \times {{10}^8}}} = 3$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: If the momentum of an electron is increased by $p_{m}$ then the de Broglie wavelength associated with it changes by $0.5 \%$. Then   find  the initial momentum of the electron is
explanation:
Let $p$ be initial momentum of electron,
$p^{\prime}=p+p_{m}$
$\therefore \quad \lambda^{\prime}=\lambda-\dfrac{0.5}{100} \lambda=\left(1-\dfrac{0.5}{100}\right) \lambda$
$\therefore \dfrac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=\dfrac{{p}}{{p}^{\prime}}$
$\therefore\left(1-\dfrac{0.5}{100}\right)=\dfrac{p}{p+p_{m}}$
$\therefore\left(\dfrac{99.5}{100}\right)=\dfrac{{p}}{{p}+{p}_{{m}}}$
$\therefore \quad 99.5 \,{p}+99.5 \,{p}_{{m}}=100 \,{p}$
$\therefore 0.5 \,{p}=99.5 \,{p}_{{m}}$
$\therefore {p}=199 \,{p}_{{m}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron in the first orbit of Bohr's hydrogen is equal to
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength ${(\lambda)}$ of an electron is given as $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{{mv}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also, from Bohr's quantization rule, the angular momentum is
${L=m v r=\dfrac{n h}{2 \pi}}$
For first Bohr's orbit ${(n=1), \lambda=2 \pi r}$ i.e., equal to the perimeter of orbit.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: If the kinetic energy of a free electron doubles, its de-Broglie wavelength changes by a factor of
explanation:
If kinetic energy of free electron ${K^{\prime} \rightarrow 2 K}$ (initial K.E.) then de-Broglie wavelength changes by
$\lambda^{\prime}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K^{\prime}}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m 2 K}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \lambda$.
So correct option is (4)

question: The momentum of a proton is ${2.5 \times 10^{-29} {~kg}-{m} / {s}}$. Its frequency will be
explanation:
Momentum ( ${p}$ ) of the proton is
$2.5 \times {10^{ - 29}}\,kg\,m/s$
Wavelength ${(\lambda)}$ of the proton will be ${\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}}$
Here, ${h=}$ Planck's constant ${=6.6 \times 10^{-34} {~J} {~s}}$
${\lambda=\dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{2.5 \times 10^{-29}} {~m}=2.64 \times 10^{-5} {~m}}$
Now, frequency ${(v)}$ of the proton will be ${v=\dfrac{c}{\lambda}=\dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.64 \times 10^{-5}}=1.14 \times 10^{13} {~Hz}}$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of momentum of an electron and $\alpha$-particle which are accelerated from rest by a potential difference of $100\,V$ is
explanation:
$P = \sqrt {2\,mKE} = \sqrt {2\,mqV} $
$\therefore \dfrac{P_{e}}{P_{\alpha}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 m_{e} q_{e} V_{e}}}{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha} V_{\alpha}}}$
Here, $V_{c}=V_{\alpha}=100, q_{\alpha}=2 q_{e}$
So, $\dfrac{P_{e}}{P_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{e}}{m_{\alpha}} \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{e}}{2 m_{\alpha}}}$
subject: Physics

question: The kinetic energy of an electron get tripled, then the de Broglie wavelength associated with it changes by a factor
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron is
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}$
where $m$ be mass of the electron and $K$ its kinetic energy.
or $\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K}}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{K^{\prime}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{3 K}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\lambda^{\prime}=\dfrac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3}}$
Hence, de Broglie wavelength changes by $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.
subject: Physics

question: The wavelength $\lambda$ of a photon and the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron have the same value. Find the ratio of energy of photon to the kinetic energy of electron in terms of mass $m$, speed of light $c$ and Planck's constant $(h)$.
explanation:
The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron,
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}} \Rightarrow v = \frac{h}{{m\lambda }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Energy of photon,
${E_p} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }\left( {{\text{since,}}\lambda \,{\text{is}}\,{\text{same}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Kinetic energy of photon to kinetic
energy of electron
$\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{{{E_p}}}{{{E_e}}} = \frac{{\frac{{hc}}{\lambda }}}{{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)m{v^2}}} = \frac{{2hc}}{{\lambda m{v^2}}}$
Substituting the value of $v$ from
Eq. (1), we get
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{E_p}}}{{{E_e}}} = \frac{{2hc}}{{\lambda m{{\left( {\frac{h}{{m\lambda }}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{2\lambda mc}}{h}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: Two particles are moving at right angle to each other, and their de Broglie wavelengths are ${\lambda_{1}}$ and ${\lambda_{2}}$, respectively. The particles suffer perfectly inelastic collision. The de Broglie wavelength ${\lambda}$ of the final particle is given by
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956561/preptracker/questions/q_45405_explanation_1764956560894.png ${\vec{p}_{1}=\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{1}} \hat{i}}$ and ${\vec{p}_{2}=\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{2}} \hat{j} n}$
Using momentum conservation,
${\vec{p}=\vec{p}_{1}+\vec{p}_{2}}$
${=\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{1}} \hat{i}+\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{2}} \hat{j}}$
${|\vec{p}|=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{2}}\right)^{2}}}$
${\dfrac{h}{\lambda}=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{2}}\right)^{2}}}$
${\dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{2}^{2}}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A particle ${P}$ is formed due to a completely inelastic collision of particles ${x}$ and ${y}$, having de-Broglie wavelengths ${\lambda_{x}}$ and ${\lambda_{y}}$ respectively. If ${x}$ and ${y}$ were moving in opposite directions, then the de Broglie wavelength of ${p}$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950818/preptracker/questions/q_45401_explanation_1764950817720.png By momentum conservation,
${p_{x}-p_{y}=p_{p}}$
${\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{x}}-\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{y}}=\dfrac{h}{\lambda_{p}}}$
${\lambda_{p}=\dfrac{\lambda_{x} \lambda_{y}}{\left|\lambda_{x}-\lambda_{y}\right|}}$
subject: Physics

question: The graph which shows the variation of ${\left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)}$ and its kinetic energy, ${E}$ is (where ${\lambda}$ is de Broglie wavelength of a free particle):
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength
${\lambda=\dfrac{h}{P}=\dfrac{h}{m v}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}}$ where ${E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}$
Squaring both sides,
$\lambda^{2}=\dfrac{h^{2}}{2 m E} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}=(\text { constant }) E$
Graph passes through origin with constant slope. Correct option is (4).

question: An $\alpha$-particle, a proton and an electron have the same kinetic energy. Which one of the following is correct in case of their de-Broglie wavelength?
explanation:
$p^{2} / 2 m=E_{k} \Rightarrow p=\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}$.
$\therefore \lambda=$ De-Broglie wavelength $=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}}$
$\propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$ for same kinetic energy $E_{k}$.
As mass of electron is minimum out of given particles, so it has largest wavelength.
And mass of alpha particle is maximum, so its de-Broglie wavelength is shortest.
Hence, $\lambda_{\alpha} < \lambda_{p} < \lambda_{e}$
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: Proton $(P)$ and electron (e) will have same de-Broglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, $m_{P}=1849 m_{e}$ ).
explanation:

Given $: \dfrac{\lambda_{\text {proton }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}}=1$
$\dfrac{h / p_{\text {proton }}}{h / p_{\text {electron }}}=1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{p_{\text {proton }}}{p_{\text {electron }}}=1$

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a molecule in a gas at room temperature $(300 K)$ is $\lambda_{1}$. If the temperature of the gas is increased to $600\,K$, then the de Broglie wavelength of the same gas molecule becomes
explanation:
$T_{1}=300 K, \lambda_{1}$
$T_{2}=600 K, \lambda_{2}=$?
de-Broglie wavelength is given by
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{P}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K E}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \times \dfrac{3}{2} K T}}$
Here $K E=$ Average $K E$ of each molecule $=\dfrac{3}{2} K T$.
$\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{300}{600}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
or $\lambda_{2}=\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\sqrt{2}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A proton moving with one tenth of velocity of light has a certain de-Broglie wavelength of $\lambda$. An alpha particle having certain kinetic energy has the same de- Broglie wavelength $\lambda$. The ratio of kinetic energy of proton and that of alpha particle is
explanation:
As $V=\dfrac{c}{10}$, we can assume that the velocity of the proton is in non - relativistic range.
$K E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\left(\dfrac{h}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2 m}=\dfrac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}$
Ratio of kinetic energy of proton and that alpha particle is $\dfrac{K E_{p}}{K E_{\alpha}}=\dfrac{m_{\mu}}{m_{p}}=\dfrac{4 m_{p}}{m_{p}}=4: 1$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The kinetic energy of an electron, $\alpha$-particle and a proton are given as $4 K, 2 K$ and $K$ respectively. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron $\left(\lambda_{e}\right), \alpha$-particle $\left(\lambda_{\alpha}\right)$ and the proton $\left(\lambda_{p}\right)$ are as follows :
explanation:
As, $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m(K E)}} ; \lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m(K E)}}$
Let mass of proton $m_{p}=1$.
For $\alpha$-particle, $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{4 \times 2 K}}$ or $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{8 K}}$
For electron, $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1840} \times 4 K}}$ or $\sqrt{\dfrac{1840}{4 K}} h$
For proton, $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{K}}$
As $\alpha$-particle is heavier than both proton and electron.
$\therefore \lambda_{\alpha} < \lambda_{p} < \lambda_{e}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron accelerated through a potential difference $V_{1}$ has a de-Broglie wavelength of $\lambda$. When the potential is changed to $V_{2}$, its deBroglie wavelength increases by $50 \%$. The value of $\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)$ is equal to
explanation:
When a charged particle of mass $m$, carrying a charge $Q$ is accelerated. Through a potential difference of $V$, its final linear momentum is given by
$\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}=Q V \Rightarrow p=\sqrt{2 m Q V}$.
$\therefore$ de Broglie wavelength $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m Q V}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{1.5}{\lambda}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{9}{4}$.
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The ratio of de-Broglie wavelength of an $\alpha$ particle and a proton accelerated from rest by the same potential is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$, the value of $m$ is
explanation:
Here: $m_{\alpha}=4 m_{P}, q_{\alpha}=2 q_{P}$,
$V=$ Potential difference.
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}$
so, $\dfrac{\lambda_{\alpha}}{\lambda_{P}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 m_{P} q_{P} V}{2 m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha} V}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{P} \cdot q_{P}}{4 m_{P} \times 2 q_{P}}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{8}}$
So, $m=8$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: Electron beam used in an electron microscope, when accelerated by a voltage of $20\,kV$, has a de-Broglie wavelength of $\lambda_{0}$. If the voltage is increased to $40\,kV$, then the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron beam would be
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength, is given by
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 M e V}}$
$\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V}}$ So, $\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{20}{40}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\lambda_{2}=\dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron having kinetic energy $E$ is $\lambda$. If the kinetic energy of electron becomes $\dfrac{E}{4}$, then its de-Broglie wavelength will be
explanation:
If $p$ be the linear momentum of a moving particle and $m$ be its mass, then its kinetic energy is given by $E=p^{2} / 2 m$
$\therefore p=\sqrt{2 m E}$.
Therefore de Broglie equation is given by
$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}} \Rightarrow \lambda \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{E}}$.
$\therefore \dfrac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{E^{\prime}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{E / 4}}=2 \Rightarrow \lambda^{\prime}=2 \lambda$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A proton and an $\alpha$-particle are accelerated from rest by $2\,V$ and $4\,V$ potentials respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is
explanation:
If a charged particle of mass $m$, carrying a charge $Q$ is accelerated through a potential difference of ' $V$ ' volt, then the kinetic energy acquired by the particle is given by
$\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}=Q \cdot V \Rightarrow u=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 Q V}{m}}$
$\therefore$ de Broglie wavelength of the charged particle is $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{m V}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 Q m V} \text {. }}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{\alpha}} \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{\alpha}}{Q_{p}}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{p}}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{\alpha}}{V_{p}}}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 e}{e}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{4 m p}{m p}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{4 V}{2 V}}=4$.
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelengths of proton and electron having same kinetic energy (Assume $m_{p}=m_{e} \times 1849$)
explanation:
Kinetic energy, of a particle of mass $m$ is given by $E_{k}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ where $v$ is the velocity of the particle.
As, $p=m v=\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}$
$\therefore$ de-broglie wavelength of the moving particle is given by, $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}}$
For proton, $\lambda_{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} E_{k}}}$; for electron,
$\lambda_{e}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} E_{k}}}$
So, $\dfrac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{e}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{m_{e}}}{\sqrt{m_{p}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{e}}{m_{e} \times 1849}}$;
$\dfrac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{e}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1849}}=\dfrac{1}{43}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question:
If the electron and proton are accelerated by same potential difference $100\,V$, then find the ratio of their de-Broglie wavelengths
${m_e} = 9.1 \times {10^{ - 31}}\,kg$
${m_p} = 1.6825 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg$
explanation:
An electron and a proton, accelerated through the same potential difference $$V$$, acquire same kinetic energy $K( = eV)$. For a particle of mass $m$ and kinetic energy $K$, de-Broglie wavelength: $$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{m v}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 K m}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 e V m}}$$
$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{p}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{p}}{m_{e}}} \approx 43 .$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength $$\left(\lambda_{B}\right)$$ associated with the electron orbiting in the second excited state of hydrogen atom is related to that in the ground state $$\left(\lambda_{G}\right)$$ by:
explanation:
$$\lambda \alpha \dfrac{1}{P} \Rightarrow \lambda \alpha \dfrac{1}{v}$$
The velocity
$$v \alpha \dfrac{1}{n} \Rightarrow \lambda \alpha n \Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{G}}=\dfrac{n_{B}}{n_{G}}=3$$
$\therefore {\lambda _B} = 3{\lambda _G}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: de-Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated by voltage of $50\,V$ is close to $\left( {|e| = 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}C,{m_e} = 9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}\,kg} \right.$, $\left. {h = 6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}Js} \right)$:
explanation:
Momentum $$P=\sqrt{2 m E}$$
$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mE} }}$
$ = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{\sqrt {2 \times 9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 50} }}$
$ = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,m = 1.7\mathop A\limits^o $
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the $n = 4$ level is :
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength of electron
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}}$
As we know, $v\,\alpha \frac{1}{n}$ So, $\lambda \,\alpha \,n \Rightarrow {\lambda _4} = 4{\lambda _1}$
$$\lambda_{1}$$ is the de Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state.

options.text: ${\frac{1}{4}^{th}}$ of the de Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Four times the de Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Two times the de Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Half of the de Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A parallel beam of electrons travelling in $x$ direction falls on a slit of width $d$ (see figure). If after passing the slit, an electron acquires momentum $$p_{y}$$ in the $y$-direction then for a majority of electrons passing through the slit ($h$ is Planck's constant) :
explanation:
$$d \sin \theta=\lambda \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\dfrac{\lambda}{d} < 1 \Rightarrow \lambda < d$$
$\frac{h}{{\left| {{p_y}} \right|}} < d \Rightarrow h < \left| {{p_y}} \right|d$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: For which of the following particles will it be most difficult to experimentally verify the de Broglie relationship?
explanation: If mass of a particle is more, then its de Broglie's wavelength is small and hence it is difficult to find $$\lambda$$ for a dust particle
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A photon of wavelength $$\lambda$$ is scattered from an electron, which was at rest. The wavelength shift $$\Delta \lambda$$ is three times of $$\lambda$$ and the angle of scattering $$\theta$$ is $$60^{\circ}$$. The angle at which the electron recoiled is $$\phi$$. The value of $$\tan \phi$$ is : (electron speed is much smaller than the speed of light)
explanation:
Given that $$\Delta \lambda=3 \lambda \Rightarrow \lambda^{\prime}=4 \lambda$$
The scattering is shown in the figure
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950796/preptracker/questions/q_44940_explanation_1764950796057.png
${m_0}v\sin \phi = \frac{h}{{{\lambda ^\prime }}}\sin \theta = \frac{h}{{4\lambda }}\sin \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$$\dfrac{h}{\lambda^{\prime}} \cos \theta+m_{0} v \cos \phi=\dfrac{h}{\lambda}$$
${m_0}v\cos \phi = \frac{h}{\lambda } - \frac{h}{{4\lambda }}\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2)
$$\tan \phi=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4} \sin \theta}{1-\dfrac{1}{4} \cos \theta}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{7} \simeq 0.25$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: If we express the energy of a photon in $KeV$ and the wavelength in angstroms, then energy of a photon can be calculated from the relation
explanation:
Energy of photon
$E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }({\text{ }}Joules{\text{ }}) = \frac{{hc}}{{e\lambda }}(eV)$
$ \Rightarrow E(eV) = \frac{\begin{gathered} 6.6 \times {10^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {10^8} \times \left( {\frac{1}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 29}}}}eV} \right) \hfill \\ \left( {\frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 10}}}}\mathop A\limits^o } \right) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} }{{\lambda (\mathop A\limits^o )}}$
$E(eV) = \frac{{12375}}{{\lambda \mathop A\limits^o }}$
$ \Rightarrow E(KeV) = \frac{{12.37}}{{\lambda (\mathop A\limits^o )}} \approx \frac{{12.4}}{\lambda }$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: If the momentum of an electron is changed by $$\Delta p$$, then the de-Broglie wavelength associated with its changes by $$0.5 \%$$. The initial momentum of electron will be:
explanation:
$$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{P}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda}=\dfrac{-\Delta P}{P} \\& \dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} \times 100=\dfrac{-\Delta P}{P} \times 100 \\& 0.5=\dfrac{-\Delta P}{P} \times 100 \\& P=-200 \Delta P \\& {[P]=200 \Delta P}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question:
The potential energy of a particle of mass $$m$$ is given by
$$U(x)= \begin{cases}E_{0}: & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 0 ; & x>1\end{cases}$$
$$\lambda_{1}$$ and $$\lambda_{2}$$ are the de Broglie wavelength of the particle, when $$0 \leq x \leq 1$$ and $$x>1$$ respectively. If the total energy of particle is $$2 E_{0}$$, the ratio $$\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}$$ will be
explanation:
$$K . E .=2 E_{0}-E_{0}=E_{0}($$ for $$0 \leq x \leq 1)$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{1}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_{0}}}$$
$$K . E .=2 E_{0}(\text { for } x>1)$$
$$\Rightarrow \lambda_{2}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{4 m E_{0}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{2}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron (mass $$m$$ ) with an initial velocity $v = {v_0}\hat i\left( {{v_0} > 0} \right)$ in an electric field $$E=E_{0} \hat{i}$$ $$\left(E_{0}=\right.$$ constant $$\left.>0\right)$$. It's de Broglie wavelength at time $$t$$ is given by
explanation:
Initial de Broglie wavelength of electron,
${\lambda _0} = \frac{h}{{m{v_0}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Force on electron in electric field,
$\overline F = - e\overline E = - e\left[ { - {E_0}\widehat i} \right] = e{E_0}\widehat i$
Acceleration of electron $a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{{e{E_0}}}{{\,m}}$
Velocity of electron after time $$t$$,
$v = {v_0} + \left( {\frac{{e{E_0}}}{{\,m}}} \right)t$
$ = \left( {{v_0} + \frac{{e{E_0}}}{{\,m}}t} \right) = {v_0}\left( {1 + \frac{{e{E_0}}}{{m{v_0}}}t} \right)$
de Broglie wavelength associated with electron at time $$t$$ is
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{m{v_0}}} = \frac{h}{{m\left[ {{v_0}\left( {1 + \frac{{e{E_0}}}{{m{v_0}}}t} \right)} \right]}} = \frac{{{\lambda _0}}}{{m\left[ {1 + \frac{{e{E_0}}}{{m{v_0}}}t} \right]}}$
[from Eq. (1)]
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A photon and electron have same de Broglie wavelength. Given that $v$ is the speed of electron and $$c$$ is the velocity of light. $$E_{e}, E_{p}$$ are the kinetic energies of electron and photon respectively. $$p_{e}, p_{h}$$ are the momentum of electron and photon respectively. Then which of the following relation is correct?
explanation:
As,
${E_e} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}(mv)v = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{h}{\lambda }} \right)v$
and ${\quad {E_p} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda };}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{E_e}}}{{{E_p}}} = \frac{v}{{2c}}$
Now, ${p_e} = mv = \frac{h}{\lambda }$
and $$\quad p_{P}=\dfrac{h}{\lambda} \therefore \dfrac{p_{e}}{p_{P}}=1$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron is 2000 times lighter than a proton. Both are moving such that their matter waves have same wavelength. The ratio of their kinetic energies in approximation is:
explanation:
Since both have same de-Broglie wavelength, hence both must have equal value of momentum.
Since $$E=\dfrac{P^{2}}{2 m}$$
$$\dfrac{E_{e}}{E_{P}}=\dfrac{m_{p}}{m_{e}}=2000$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A particle of mass $$M$$ at rest decays into two particles of masses $$m_{1}$$ and $$m_{2}$$, having non-zero velocities. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelengths of the particles, $$\lambda_{1} / \lambda_{2}$$, is
explanation:
de-Broglie wavelength
$$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p} \therefore \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}$$
And momentum is conserved in decay so $$p_{1}=p_{2}$$ and $$\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=1$$.
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: If the kinetic energy of the particle is increased to 16 times its previous value, the percentage change in the de Broglie wavelength of the particle is
explanation:
For de Broglie wavelength,
$$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}$$
For ${1^{st}}$ case, $$\quad \lambda_{1}=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}$$
For ${2^{nd}}$ case, $$\lambda_{2}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m 16 K}}=\dfrac{h}{4 \sqrt{2 m K}}=\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{4}$$
So, $$75 \%$$ change in the wavelength takes place.
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: de Broglie wavelength of a body of mass $1\,kg$ moving with velocity of $2000\,m/s$ is
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The figure shows standing de Broglie waves due to the revolvtion of electron in a certain orbit of hydrogen atom. Then the expression for the orbit radius is (all notations have their ususal meanings)
explanation:
Number of standing waves in the orbit $$=n=6$$ (from the given figure)
Radius of orbit with principal quantum number $n$,
${r_n} = \frac{{{n^2}{h^2}{\varepsilon _0}}}{{m{e^2}\pi }} = \frac{{36{h^2}{\varepsilon _0}}}{{\pi m{e^2}}}$
None of the options match the correct answer.
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A proton and an $$\alpha$$ particle are accelerated through the same potential difference $V$. The ratio of their de Broglie wavelength is
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength of a particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mqV} }}$
Since the potential difference $V$ is the same for both the particles,
$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt {mq} }}$
Thus
$$\dfrac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}{m_{p} q_{p}}}=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{4 m_{p}}{m_{p}}\right)\left(\dfrac{2 e}{e}\right)}=\dfrac{\sqrt{8}}{1}=2 \sqrt{2}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A particle is dropped from a height ' $$H$$ '. The de Broglie wavelength of the particle depends on height as
explanation:
When the particle is dropped from height $H$, then the velocity at lowest point is $v = \sqrt {2gH} $
de Broglie's wavelength $\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \frac{h}{{\,m\sqrt {2gH} }}$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{h}{{\sqrt H }}$ or $\lambda \propto {H^{^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: According to de - Brogile hypothesis, the wavelength associated with moving electron of mass ' $m$ ' is $$\lambda_{e}$$. Using mass energy relation and Planck's quantum theory, the wavelength associated with photon is $$\lambda_{p}$$. If the energy ($E$) electron and photon is same, then relation between $$\lambda_{e}$$ and $$\lambda_{p}$$ is
explanation:
The energy of photon is given as
${E_p} = \frac{{hc}}{{{\lambda _p}}}$
$\therefore {\lambda _p} = \frac{{hc}}{{{E_p}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Energy of an moving electron is given as
${E_e} = m{c^2} = pc \Rightarrow p = \frac{{{E_p}}}{c}$
$\therefore {\lambda _e} = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{{hc}}{{{E_e}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Given, $$E_{p}=E_{e}$$
Therefore, from eq.(1) and eq.(2), we get $$\lambda_{p} \propto \lambda_{e}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A proton and an alpha particle are accelerated under the same potential difference. The ratio of de - Broglie wavelengths of the proton and the alpha particle is
explanation:
When a charged particle of charge $$q$$ and mass $$m$$ is accelerated under a potential difference $V$, let $$v$$ be velocity acquired by the particle
Then
$qV = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2} \Rightarrow mv = \sqrt {2mqV} $
$$\therefore$$ de Broglie wavlength $\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mqV} }}$
$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt {mq} }}$ for the same value of $V$
$\therefore \frac{{{\lambda _p}}}{{{\lambda _\alpha }}} = \frac{{\sqrt {{m_\alpha }{q_\alpha }} }}{{\sqrt {{m_p}{q_p}} }} = \sqrt {\frac{{4\,m}}{{\,m}} \times \frac{{2e}}{e}} = \sqrt 8 $
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: If an electron and a proton have the same de-Broglie wavelength, then the kinetic energy of the electron is
explanation:
Using $$E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$$ and $$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}$$,
$$\begin{gathered}\quad E=\dfrac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}} \\\therefore \text { For same } \lambda, \dfrac{E_{e}}{E_{p}}=\dfrac{m_{p}}{m_{e}},\end{gathered}$$
As ${m_p} > > {m_e},{E_e} > > {E_p}$.

options.text: More than that of a proton
Equal to that of a proton
Less than that of a proton.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: What is the de Broglie wavelength of the electron accelerated through a potential difference of 100 $volt$?
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength of the electron accelerated through a potential difference of $V$ volt is
$\lambda = \frac{{12.27}}{{\sqrt V }}\mathop A\limits^o $
For $V = 100\,volt$
$\lambda = \frac{{12.27}}{{\sqrt {100} }}\mathop A\limits^o = 1.227\mathop A\limits^o $
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de Broglie wavelength associated with a particle of mass $m$, moving with a velocity $v$ and energy $E$ is given by
explanation: $\lambda  = \frac{h}{P} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mE} }}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The energy of an electron having de - Brogile wavelength $$\lambda$$ is ( where $$h=$$ Planck's constant, $$\mathrm{m}=$$ mass of electron)
explanation:
We know that
$$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m(K E)}}$$
on taking square on both sides, we get
${\lambda ^2} = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2m(KE)}}$
$KE = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2m{\lambda ^2}}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de - Brogile wavelength $$\lambda$$ of a particle
explanation:
de - Brogile wavelength $$(\lambda)$$ of a particle is related to its momentum ($p$) as $$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}$$
where, $h$ is Planck's constant
${\text{ or }}\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}}\quad [\because p = mv\} $
$\quad \lambda = \frac{h}{{m\frac{v}{t}.t}} = \frac{h}{{ma \cdot t}} = \frac{h}{{F \cdot t}}$
$[\because F = ma]$
$(\lambda )$ is inversely proportional to impulse $$(F)$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron of mass $$m_{e}$$ and a proton of mass ${m_p}$ are moving with the same speed. The ratio of their de - Broglie's wavelength $$\dfrac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{p}}$$ is
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength $\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}}$
where $m$ is the mass and $$v$$ is the speed of the particle.
As electron and proton both are moving with same speed, therefore the ratio of their de Broglie wavelengths is
$\frac{{{\lambda _e}}}{{{\lambda _p}}} = \frac{{{m_p}}}{{{m_e}}} = \frac{{1.6726 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}\,kg}}{{9.1094 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}\,kg}} \approx 1836$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron of mass $$m$$ has de - Brogile wavelength $$\lambda$$ when accelerated through potential difference $V$. When proton of mass $M$, is accelerated through potential difference $9\,V$, the de - Brogile wavelength associated with it will be (assume that wavelength is determined at low voltage)
explanation:
According to question,
Mass of electron $ = m$
Wavelength of electron $$=\lambda$$
Potential difference in case of electron $ = V$
From de - Brogile relation,
$$\begin{aligned}& \lambda=\dfrac{h}{p} \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K E}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}} \\& \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{q V m}}\end{aligned}$$
For electron, $\lambda \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt {eVm} }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For proton, ${\lambda _1} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {e9VM} }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Where, $e$ is the charge on proton, potential differnce $ = 9\,V$
Mass of proton $ = m$
From eq.(1) and (2), we get
$$\dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{9 V M e}{e V m}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{1}=\dfrac{\lambda}{3} \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{M}}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated to a potential of $400\,V$ is approximately
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2meV} }}$
here, $V = 400\,V$
$\lambda = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{\sqrt {2 \times 9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 400} }}$
$ \approx 0.06 \times {10^{ - 9}}\,m = 0.06\,nm$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The additional energy that should be given to an electron to reduce its de - Broglie wavelength from $1\,nm$ to $0.5\,nm$ is
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength of an electron having kinetic energy $K$ is
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2\,{m_e}K} }}$ where ${m_e}$ is the mass of an electron
$\therefore \quad {\lambda ^2} = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2\,{m_e}K}}{\rm{ }}or{\rm{ }}K = \frac{h}{{2\,{m_e}{\lambda ^2}}} \Rightarrow K \propto \frac{1}{{{\lambda ^2}}}$
$\therefore \frac{{{K^\prime }}}{K} = {\left( {\frac{\lambda }{{{\lambda ^\prime }}}} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{{1\,nm}}{{0.5\,nm}}} \right)^2} = 4$
${K^\prime } = 4K$
Additional energy given $$=$$
${K^\prime } - K = 4\,K - K = 3\,K$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The kinetic energy of an electron gets tripled then the de Broglie wavelength associated with it changes by a factor
explanation:
For an electron,
de Broglie wavelength $\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mK} }}$
where $h = $ Planck's constant, $m = $ mass of an electron, $k = $ kinetic energy of an electron
Since $$h$$ and $$m$$ are constants, $\lambda \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt K }}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda }{{{\lambda ^\prime }}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{K^\prime }}}{K}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3\,K}}{k}} {\rm{ or }}{\lambda ^\prime } = \frac{\lambda }{{\sqrt 3 }}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The graph which shows the variation of the de Broglie wavelength $$(\lambda)$$ of a particle and its associated momentum (\[p\]) is
explanation:
$$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}$$
Graph will be hyperbolic
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_08_28_2f6278dbe11abe6eee1cg-13.jpg?height=320&width=307&top_left_y=1590&top_left_x=1348
Note: If it is to be linear, it is to be of $$y=m x$$ type
or $$y=m x+c$$ type
Here $$y=\lambda, x=p$$
But we have $$\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{p}$$
$$\Rightarrow$$ Curved as in (3)
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electromagnetic wave of wavelength ' $$\lambda$$ ', is incident on a photosensitive surface of negligible work function. If ' $m$ ' is the mass of photoelectron emitted from the surface has deBroglie wavelength ' $$\lambda_{d}$$ ' then
explanation:
$$\dfrac{h c}{\lambda}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\phi$$
Given that $$\phi \approx 0$$
$$\dfrac{h c}{\lambda}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}=\dfrac{P^{2}}{2 m} \\& P=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 m h c}{\lambda}} \\& \lambda_{d}=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2 m h c}{\lambda}}} \\& =\sqrt{\dfrac{h^{2} \lambda}{2 m h c}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{h \lambda}{2 m c}} \\& \lambda_{d}^{2}=\dfrac{h \lambda}{2 m c} \Rightarrow \lambda=\dfrac{2 m c}{h} \lambda_{d}^{2}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference of $$ volt. If the de Broglie wavelength of the electron is $1.227 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,nm$, the potential difference is:
explanation:
$\lambda = \frac{{12.27}}{{\sqrt V }}\mathop A\limits^o $
$\sqrt v = {10^2} \Rightarrow v = {10^4}{\rm{ }}volt{\rm{ }}$
subject: Physics

question: An electron is accelerated through a potential difference of 10,000 $V$. Its de Broglie wavelength is, (nearly) : $\left( {{m_e} = 9 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}\,kg} \right)$
explanation:
For an electron accelerated through a potential $V$
$\lambda = \frac{{12.27}}{{\sqrt V }}\mathop A\limits^o = \frac{{12.27 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{\sqrt {10000} }} = 12.27 \times {10^{ - 12}}\,m$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron of mass $$m$$ with an initial velocity $\overrightarrow v  = {v_0}\hat i\left( {{v_0} > 0} \right)$ enters an electric field $\vec E =  - {E_0}\hat i\left( {{E_0} = } \right.$ constant $$\left.>0\right)$$ at $$\mathrm{t}=0$$. If $$\lambda_{0}$$ is its de-Broglie wavelength initially, then its de-Broglie wavelength at time $t$ is
explanation:
$$\vec{F}=q \vec{E}=e E_{0} \hat{i}$$
$\vec a = \frac{{\vec F}}{m} = \frac{{e{E_0}}}{m}\hat i$
$v = {v_0} + at$
$v = {v_0} + \frac{{e{E_0}}}{m}t$
$P = m{v_0} + e{E_0}t$
$\lambda = \frac{h}{P} = \frac{h}{{m{v_0} + e{E_0}t}} = \frac{{\frac{h}{{m{v_0}}}}}{{1 + \frac{{e{E_0}t}}{{m{v_0}}}}} = \frac{{{\lambda _0}}}{{1 + \frac{{e{E_0}}}{{m{v_0}}}t}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de Broglie wavelength of a neutron in thermal equilibrium with heavy water at a temperature $\,T$ (Kelvin) and mass $\,m$, is :-
explanation:
Kinetic energy of thermal neutron with equilibrium is $\frac{3}{2}KT$
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2\,m\,K \cdot E} }} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2\,m\left( {\frac{3}{2}KT} \right)} }} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {3mkT} }}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron of mass $$m$$ and $a$ photon have same energy $E$. The ratio of de Broglie wavelengths associated with them is :
explanation:
For electron $$\lambda_{e}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}$$
for Photon $$E=P c \Rightarrow \lambda_{P h}=\dfrac{h c}{E}$$
$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{P h}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}} \times \dfrac{E}{h c}=\left(\dfrac{E}{2 m}\right)^{1 / 2} \dfrac{1}{c}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question:
Assertion: A wave is a moving, dynamic disturbance of matter or energy in an organised and periodic way.

explanation: Option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: The de Broglie wavelength of a molecule varies inversely as the square root of temperature.


Reason: The root mean square velocity of the molecule depends on the temperature.
explanation:
de Broglie wavelength associated with gas molecule varies as
$$\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$
Also, root mean square velocity of gas molecule is,
${v_{{\rm{rms}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3RT}}{M}} $
Momentum $$p=\dfrac{h}{\lambda}=m v_{r m s}$$.
So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: A proton, a neutron, an electron and an $$\alpha$$-particle have same energy. Then, their de Broglie wavelength compare as
explanation:
We know that the relation between $$\lambda$$ and $E$ is given by
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mE} }}$
Here, for the given value of energy $E,\frac{h}{{\sqrt {2E} }}$ is a constant.
Thus, $\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt m }}$
$\therefore {\lambda _p}:{\lambda _n}:{\lambda _e}:{\lambda _\alpha } = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{m_p}} }}:\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{m_n}} }}:\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{m_e}} }}:\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{m_\alpha }} }}$
Since, $$m_{p}=m_{n}$$, hence $$\lambda_{p}=\lambda_{n}$$
As, ${m_\alpha } > {m_p}$, therefore ${\lambda _\alpha } < {\lambda _p}$
As, ${m_e} < {m_n}$, therefore ${\lambda _e} > {\lambda _n}$
Hence, $$\lambda_{\alpha} < \lambda_{p}=\lambda_{n} < \lambda_{\text {e }}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The wavelength of a photon needed to remove a proton from a nucleus which is bound to the nucleus with $1M\,eV$ energy is nearly
explanation:
Given in the equation,
Energy of a photon, $E = 1MeV \Rightarrow {10^6}eV$
Now, $hc$ $ = 1240\,eVnm$
Now, $$E=\dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E} = \frac{{1240eVnm}}{{{{10}^6}eV}} = 1.24 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,nm$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron is moving with an initial velocity $v = {v_0}\widehat i$ in a magnetic field $B = {B_0}j$. Then it's de Broglie wavelength
explanation: Here, when a particle is moving in a magnetic field then its speed remains constant. So the magnitude of $$v$$ will not change, i.e., momentum ( $mv$ ) will remain constant in magnitude. Hence de Broglie wavelength $\lambda  = h/mv$ remains constant.
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron (of mass $$m$$ ) with an initial velocity $v = {v_0}\widehat i$ is moving in an electric field $E = {E_0}\widehat j$. If ${\lambda _0} = h/m{v_0}$, it's de Broglie wavelength at time $$t$$ is given by
explanation:
Initial de Broglie wavelength of electron,
${\lambda _0} = \frac{h}{{m{v_0}}}$
Force on electron in electric field,
$\bar F = - e\bar E = - e{E_0}\widehat j$
Acceleration of electron, $\bar a = \frac{{\bar F}}{{\,m}} = - \frac{{e{E_0}}}{{\,m}}\widehat j$
It is acting along negative $$Y$$-axis.
The initial velocity of electron along $$X$$-axis
${v_{{X_0}}} = {v_0}\widehat i.{\rm{ }}$
Initial velocity of electron along $$Y$$-axis,
${v_{{Y_0}}} = 0$.
Velocity of electron after time $$t$$ along $$X$$-axis,
${v_X} = {v_0}\widehat i$
( $$\therefore$$ There is no acceleration of electron along $$X$$-axis )
Velocity of electron after time $$t$$ along $$Y$$-axis,
${v_Y} = 0 + \left( { - \frac{{e{E_0}}}{m}\widehat j} \right)t = - \frac{{e{E_0}}}{m}t\widehat j$
Magnitude of velocity of electron after time $$t$$ is
$|v| = \sqrt {v_X^2 + v_Y^2} = \sqrt {v_0^2 + {{\left( {\frac{{ - e{E_0}}}{m}t} \right)}^2}} $
$ = {v_0}\sqrt {1 + \frac{{{e^2}E_0^2{t^2}}}{{{m^2}v_0^2}}} $
de Broglie wavelength,
${\lambda ^\prime } = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \frac{h}{{m{v_0}\sqrt {1 + {e^2}E_0^2{t^2}/\left( {{m^2}v_0^2} \right)} }}$
$ = \frac{{{\lambda _0}}}{{\sqrt {1 + {e^2}E_0^2{t^2}/\left( {{m^2}v_0^2} \right)} }}$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: What is the de Broglie wavelength of a nitrogen molecule in air at $300\,K$ ? Assume that the molecule is moving with the root-mean-square speed of molecules at this temperature.(Atomic mass of nitrogen $ = 14.0076\,u$ )
explanation:
Given, temperature $T = 300\,K$
and Boltzmann's constant $ = 1.38 \times {10^{ - 23}}\,J/k$
Molecular weight of nitrogen molecule
$m = 28.0152\,u$
$ = 28.0152 \times 1.67 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg$
Mean kinetic energy of molecules
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{3}{2}KT$
or $\quad v = \sqrt {\frac{{3KT}}{m}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{3 \times 1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 300}}{{28.0152 \times 1.66 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}} $
$ = 516.78\,m/s$
de Broglie wavelength,
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}}$
$\lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{28.0152 \times 1.67 \times {{10}^{ - 27}} \times 516.78}} = 0.028\,nm$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron of mass $$m$$ and charge $e$ initially at rest gets accelerated by a constant electric field $$E$$. The rate of change of de Broglie wavelength of this electron at time $$t$$ ignoring relativistic effect is
explanation:
Here, $$u=0 ; a=\dfrac{e E}{m}$$;
$\therefore \quad v = u + at = 0 + \frac{{eE}}{m}t$ (from equation of motion) de Broglie wavelength,
$\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}} = \frac{h}{{m(eEt/m)}} = \frac{h}{{eEt}}$
Rate of change of de Broglie wavelength,
$$\dfrac{d \lambda}{d t}=\dfrac{h}{e E}\left(-\dfrac{1}{t^{2}}\right)=\dfrac{-h}{e E t^{2}}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron and a photon, each has a wavelength of $1.2\mathop A\limits^o $. What is the ratio of their energies?
explanation:
$$\dfrac{E_{e}}{E_{p h}}=\dfrac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}} \times \dfrac{\lambda}{h c}=\dfrac{h}{2 m \lambda c}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \\& =\dfrac{1}{100}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: If the kinetic energy of the particle is increased by 16 times, the percentage change in the de-Broglie wavelength of the particle is:
explanation:
For de-Broglie wavelength,
$$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}} \Rightarrow \lambda \alpha \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K}}$$
Hence, $$\lambda_{2}=\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{4}$$
$$\dfrac{\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1}} \times 100=75 \%$$
There is $$75 \%$$ change in the wavelength.
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The ratio of the respective de-Broglie wavelength associated with electrons accelerated from rest with the voltages $100\,V,\,200\,V$ and $300\,V$ is
explanation:
As we know, de-Broglie wavelength
$$\begin{gathered}\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}} \text { or } \lambda=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V}} \\\therefore \lambda_{1}: \lambda_{2}: \lambda_{3}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V_{1}}}: \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V_{2}}}: \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V_{3}}} \\=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{100}}: \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{200}}: \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{300}} \\=1: \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}: \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron and the wavelength of a photon are the same. The ratio between the energy of the photon and the momentum of electron is ($c$ = velocity of light, $h$ = Planck's constant):
explanation:
For photon, $$E=\dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$
But $$\lambda_{\text {Photon }}=\lambda_{e}=\dfrac{h}{P_{e}}\left(\lambda_{\text {Photon }}=\lambda\right)$$
$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{E}{P_{e}}=\dfrac{h c / \lambda}{h / \lambda}=c$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The de Broglie wavelength of a neutron at $27^\circ C$ is $$\lambda_{0}$$. What will be its wavelength at $$927^{\circ} C$$ ?
explanation:
The de Broglie wavelength associated with it is,
$$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \times \dfrac{3}{2} k T}}$$
i.e., $$\quad \lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$
$$\therefore \quad \dfrac{\lambda_{927}}{\lambda_{27}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{27+273}{927+273}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{300}{1200}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$$
$$\Rightarrow \lambda_{927}=\dfrac{\lambda_{27}}{2}=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: The ratio of de-Broglie wavelengths of molecules of hydrogen and helium which are at temperature $27^\circ C$ and $127^\circ C$ respectively is
explanation:
Wavelength $\lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {3mKT} }}$
$ = \frac{{{\lambda _H}}}{{{\lambda _{He}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{m_{He}}{T_{He}}}}{{{m_H}{T_H}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{4(273 + 127)}}{{2(273 + 27)}}} = \sqrt {\frac{8}{3}} $
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

question: An electron of mass $$m$$ when accelerated through a potential difference has de Broglie wavelength $$\lambda$$. The de Broglie wavelength associated with a proton of mass $M$ accelerated through the same potential difference will be
explanation:
As, $$\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$$
where, $$m=$$ mass of the charge
$$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{M}{m}} \\& \Rightarrow \lambda_{2}=\lambda \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{M}} \quad\left(\because \lambda_{1}=\lambda\right)\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
